did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning techniques, specifically convolutional neural networks (CNNs) including VGG16, VGG19, ResNet50, ResNet101, MobileNetV2, and Xception, to classify dermoscopic images of melanoma (malignant vs. benign) using a dataset of 10,000 images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the application of deep learning techniques (VGG16, VGG19, ResNet50, ResNet101, MobileNetV2, and Xception) to a dataset of 10,000 dermoscopic images for melanoma detection. It includes a methodology section, dataset description, results with performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, etc.), and a conclusion, which are characteristic of a research study rather than a review. Although it contains a literature survey section, its primary purpose is to report new experimental results.

title: Comparative Analysis Based Melanoma Detection In Dermoscopic Images With Deep Learning Techniques
journal: 2023 Fifth International Conference on Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (ICECCT)
brief_summary: This study performs a comparative analysis of deep learning techniques for detecting melanoma in dermoscopic images to classify them as malignant or benign. The researchers utilized a dataset of 10,000 images from Kaggle, split into 9,600 for training and 1,000 for evaluation. Six convolutional neural network architectures were evaluated: VGG16, VGG19, ResNet50, ResNet101, MobileNetV2, and Xception. The models were assessed using performance metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, and F-measure. The results indicated that VGG16 achieved the highest accuracy of 90% among the tested networks. The study concludes that deep learning techniques can effectively and efficiently predict melanoma, reducing manual consumption.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies dermoscopic images into malignant (melanoma) and benign categories. The dataset contains 4,605 malignant images for training and 500 for testing.

The study classifies dermoscopic images into malignant and benign categories. The dataset contains 5,000 benign images for training and 500 for testing. The paper discusses 'Melanocytic Nevi' and 'Melanoma Nevi' in the introduction and classification sections as benign neoplasms.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification of dermoscopic images into 'Malignant' (melanoma) and 'Benign' (nevi) classes. The models (VGG16, VGG19, ResNet50, ResNet101, MobileNetV2, Xception) predict discrete labels. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, F-measure, and ROC curves.

datasets_used.name: Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of 10,000 dermoscopic images obtained from the Kaggle website, containing two classes: malignant and benign.

use.training: Used to train six deep learning models (VGG16, VGG19, ResNet50, ResNet101, MobileNetV2, Xception).
use.testing: Used for evaluation of the models (referred to as 'evaluation' or 'test' set).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared model performance using descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, etc.) without statistical inference.

Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset (Kaggle).notes: VGG16 achieved the highest accuracy of 90%. The paper notes that deep learning techniques can effectively predict melanoma, reducing manual consumption. ROC curves were generated for all networks.

architecture_used.main_model: VGG16, VGG19, ResNet50, ResNet101, MobileNetV2, Xception

conclusions: VGG16 achieved the highest accuracy of 90% among the six tested deep learning architectures (VGG16, VGG19, ResNet50, ResNet101, MobileNetV2, and Xception) for melanoma detection.
Deep learning techniques can effectively and efficiently predict melanoma, significantly reducing time and manual consumption compared to traditional methods.
The study demonstrates that deep learning models can classify dermoscopic images into malignant and benign categories with varying performance metrics across different architectures.
The authors recommend using Lesion Feature Network (LIN) and Lesion Inception Feature (LIF) for future melanoma detection to address dermoscopic feature extraction.
The proposed work serves as a standard for succeeding associated studies in the field of automated melanoma detection.
important_remarks: The dataset is a small, non-standard Kaggle collection (10,000 images) rather than a recognized benchmark like ISIC, limiting generalizability and external validation potential.
The dataset exhibits class imbalance, with 5,000 benign images versus 4,605 malignant images in the training set, which may bias model performance.

Reported performance metrics contain significant errors or implausible values: F1 scores are reported as large integers (e.g., 48881.0) instead of values between 0 and 1; Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) values exceed the theoretical maximum of 1 (e.g., 1.5589); and Positive/Negative Predictive Values are negative, indicating calculation or reporting flaws.
Sensitivity (Recall) values are critically low (0.42–0.456), indicating the models fail to detect more than half of the malignant cases, which is a severe clinical limitation for cancer screening.
The study lacks formal statistical hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, or significance analysis to support the comparative claims between architectures.
No data leakage prevention strategies (e.g., patient-level splitting) or external validation on independent datasets are reported, raising concerns about overfitting to the specific Kaggle dataset.
The paper recommends future work using 'Lesion Feature Network (LIN)' and 'Lesion Inception Feature (LIF)' but does not implement or evaluate these proposed methods in the current study.
The study was conducted on Google Colab with minimal training epochs (10-20), which may be insufficient for complex CNN architectures like ResNet101 to converge optimally.
